content: Two bodies A and B have their moments of inertia I and 3I respectively about their axis of rotation. their kinetic energies of rotation are also equal. The ratio of angular momenta of body A to that of body B is
explanation:
$$ \begin{array}{ll} & \text { (K.E.) }{ }_A=(\text { K.E. })_B \\ & \frac{1}{2} I_1 \omega_1^2=\frac{1}{2} I_2 \omega_2^2 \\ \therefore & \frac{\omega_2^2}{\omega_1^2}=\frac{I_1}{I_2}=\frac{1}{3 I} \\ \therefore & \frac{\omega_2}{\omega_1}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,....(i)\end{array} $$

$$ \begin{array}{lll} & \text { Also, K.E }=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~L} \omega & \{\because \mathrm{~L}=\mathrm{I} \omega\} \\ \therefore & \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~L}_1 \omega_1=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~L}_2 \omega_2 & \\ \therefore & \frac{\mathrm{~L}_1}{\mathrm{~L}_2}=\frac{\omega_2}{\omega_1}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} & \ldots . \text { (ii) } \\ \therefore & \frac{\mathrm{L}_1}{\mathrm{~L}_2}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} & \ldots .[\text { From (i) and (ii) }] \end{array} $$

content: Four solid spheres each of mass ' $m$ ' and radius ' $r$ ' are arranged as shown in the figure. The moment of inertia of the system about the given axis of rotation $\mathrm{AA}^1$ is
explanation:
Moment of inertia of a solid sphere about an axis passing through the centre is given by,

$$ \mathrm{I}_0=\frac{2}{5} \mathrm{mr}^2 $$

Moment of inertia about a tangent is given by

$$ \mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{t}}=\frac{2}{5} \mathrm{mr}^2+\mathrm{mr}^2+\mathrm{mr}^2=\frac{7}{5} \mathrm{mr}^2 $$

In the given arrangement of the spheres, there are two spheres rotating about axis passing through the centre and two spheres are rotating about a tangent.

Hence the moment of inertia of the system is,

$$ I=\frac{2}{5} m r^2+\frac{2}{5} m r^2+\frac{7}{5} m r^2+\frac{7}{5} m r^2=\frac{18}{5} m r^2 $$

content: Four thin rods of same mass M and same length L form a square as shown in figure. Moment of inertia of this system about an axis passing through point O and perpendicular to its plane is
explanation:
Moment of inertia of the system about an axis passing through O and perpendicular to its plane

$$ \begin{aligned} I_0 & =4\left[M \operatorname{lof} \operatorname{rod}+m\left(\frac{L}{2}\right)^2\right] \\ & =4\left[\frac{M L^2}{12}+\frac{M L^2}{4}\right] \\ & =\frac{4}{3} M L^2 \end{aligned} $$

content: A solid sphere and a ring have equal mass and equal radius of gyration. If sphere is rotating about its diameter and ring about an axis passing through centre and perpendicular to its plane, then the ratio of radius of sphere to that of ring is $\sqrt{\frac{x}{2}}$ then the value of ' $x$ ' is
explanation:
Given:

  • Mass of solid sphere = mass of ring = $M$

  • Radius of gyration of sphere = radius of gyration of ring = $K$

  • Radius of sphere = $R_s$

  • Radius of ring = $R_r$

  • Both are rotating about their respective axes through the center.

Moment of Inertia:

  • For a solid sphere (about its diameter):

    $$ I_{\text{sphere}} = \frac{2}{5}MR_s^2 $$

    So, its radius of gyration $K_s$:

    $$ K_s^2 = \frac{I_{\text{sphere}}}{M} = \frac{2}{5}R_s^2 $$

  • For a ring (about center, perpendicular to its plane):

    $$ I_{\text{ring}} = MR_r^2 $$

    Radius of gyration $K_r$:

    $$ K_r^2 = \frac{I_{\text{ring}}}{M} = R_r^2 $$

Setting the radii of gyration equal:

$$ K_s = K_r $$

$$ \sqrt{\frac{2}{5}R_s^2} = R_r $$

Squaring both sides,

$$ \frac{2}{5}R_s^2 = R_r^2 $$

So,

$$ \frac{R_s^2}{R_r^2} = \frac{5}{2} $$

Taking square root:

$$ \frac{R_s}{R_r} = \sqrt{\frac{5}{2}} $$

Comparing with the question: $\frac{R_s}{R_r} = \sqrt{\frac{x}{2}}$

Therefore, $$x = 5$$

Final answer:

Option C (5)

content: Two bodies A and B have their moments of inertia ' I ' and ' 2 I ' respectively about their axis of rotation. If their kinetic energy of rotation are equal then angular momentum of body A to that of body $B$ will be in the ratio
explanation:
Given:

  • Moment of inertia of body A = $I$

  • Moment of inertia of body B = $2I$

  • Rotational kinetic energies are equal.

Let

  • Angular velocity of A = $\omega_A$

  • Angular velocity of B = $\omega_B$

  • Angular momentum of A = $L_A$

  • Angular momentum of B = $L_B$

Step 1: Write the formula for rotational kinetic energy

The kinetic energy of a rotating body is:

$$ K = \frac{1}{2}I\omega^2 $$

For both bodies,

$$ \frac{1}{2} I \omega_A^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 2I \times \omega_B^2 $$

Divide both sides by $\frac{1}{2} I$:

$$ \omega_A^2 = 2 \omega_B^2 $$

So,

$$ \omega_A = \sqrt{2} \, \omega_B $$

Step 2: Write the formula for angular momentum

Angular momentum: $L = I\omega$

Therefore,

  • $L_A = I \omega_A$

  • $L_B = 2I \omega_B$

Step 3: Find the ratio $\dfrac{L_A}{L_B}$

$$ \frac{L_A}{L_B} = \frac{I \omega_A}{2I \omega_B} = \frac{\omega_A}{2\omega_B} $$

Substitute $\omega_A = \sqrt{2}\, \omega_B$:

$$ \frac{L_A}{L_B} = \frac{\sqrt{2}\, \omega_B}{2\omega_B} = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} $$

So, the ratio $L_A : L_B = 1 : \sqrt{2}$.

Answer:

Option D: $1:\sqrt{2}$

content: A thin rod of length ' 4 L ' and mass ' 4 m ' is bent at the points as shown in the figure. The moment of inertia of the rod about an axis passing through point ' $O$ ' and perpendicular to plane of the paper is
explanation:
After bending, length of each segment is ' $L$ ' as shown in figure.

Consider only the system of two segments forming the V shape (viz., $\mathrm{OAB})$

For the axis passing through O and perpendicular to the plane of paper,

M.I. of $\operatorname{rod} \mathrm{OA}=\frac{\mathrm{mL}^2}{3}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)$

Now, distance of centre of rod AB , from O is,

$$ \begin{aligned} \mathrm{h} & =\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{L}^2}{4}+\mathrm{L}^2} \\ & =\sqrt{\frac{5 \mathrm{~L}^2}{4}} \\ & =\frac{\sqrt{5} \mathrm{~L}}{2} \end{aligned} $$

Using theorem of parallel, axes,

$$ \begin{aligned} \text { M.I. of } \mathrm{AB} & =\frac{\mathrm{mL}^2}{12}+\mathrm{mh}^2 \\ & =\frac{\mathrm{mL}^2}{12}+\frac{5 \mathrm{~mL}^2}{4} \\ & =\frac{4 \mathrm{~mL}^2}{3} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(ii)\end{aligned} $$

From equations (i) and (ii),

$$ \begin{array}{ll} & \mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{OAB}}=\frac{\mathrm{mL}^2}{3}+\frac{4 \mathrm{~mL}^2}{3}=\frac{5 \mathrm{~mL}^2}{3} \\ & \text { Similarly, } \mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{OCD}}=\frac{5 \mathrm{~mL}^2}{3} \\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{net}}=\frac{10 \mathrm{~mL}^2}{3} \end{array} $$

content: Four point masses, each of mass ' $m$ ' are arranged in $X-Y$ plane as shown in the figure. The moment of inertia of this system about $X$-axis is
explanation:
We want the moment of inertia about the X-axis for four point masses, each of mass \$m\$, located at y = 0, y = a, y = −2a, and y = 0.

The formula for point masses is $I = \sum m_i y_i^2$, where each mass is multiplied by the square of its distance from the axis.

Substitute each mass and its y-coordinate into the formula:

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { M.I. of the system about the } \mathrm{X} \text {-axis is }\\ &\begin{aligned} \mathrm{I} &=\mathrm{m} \times(0)^2+\mathrm{m} \times(\mathrm{a})^2+\mathrm{m} \times(-2 \mathrm{a})^2+\mathrm{m} \times(0)^2 \\ &=\mathrm{ma}^2+4 \mathrm{ma}^2 \\ &=5 \mathrm{ma}^2 \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

Adding these terms gives \$m a^2 + 4 m a^2 = 5 m a^2\$. Therefore, the moment of inertia is \$5 m a^2\$.
